paragraphs.text: I welcome the Commissions goal of outlawing dark patterns and other unfair online practices. Nevertheless, the draft Digital Fairness Act (DFA) underestimates the scale and specificity of fairness problems in the videogame sector, a market now worth25billion in the EU, 83% of which is fully digital. 1. Right to enjoy what weve paid for Publishers increasingly disable onlineonly titles or serverlocked singleplayer games, rendering legitimate purchases useless. Ubisofts removal of TheCrew triggered the citizenled StopKillingGames initiative, which has surpassed 1million certified signatures. The DFA should therefore introduce continuityofservice obligations: either maintain the servers/software for a reasonable period or supply a fullyfunctional offline version / unlock code so consumers retain lasting access. This parallels productlifespan rules in the Ecodesign Directive for electronics. 2. Endoflife transparency Add a duty to disclose, at the point of sale, the minimum support period and any technical dependencies (e.g., alwayson DRM, mandatory accounts). Without such information, consent cannot be freely given or informed, contrary to the GDPR and UCPD principles. 3. Fairness in virtual currencies & loot boxes Adopt the CPCNetworks March2025 key principles on ingame currency: clear pricing, no blind dualconversion, and odds disclosure for randomised items. Require a unified, oneclick cancellation / spendingcap dashboard across platforms, reflecting consumerlaw findings that current UI flows are needlessly complex. 4. Harmonise with other files Coordinate with the DSA Art25 darkpattern ban and upcoming influencermarketing code to avoid overlaps. Align with the European Accessibility Act (fromJune2025) so that any preservation tools are accessible to disabled players. 5. Why list video games expressly Sectorspecific wording is common in EU consumer law (e.g., Directive2019/770 on digital content). Naming video games in Art2 DFA would send a clear compliance signal and prevent the common misinterpretation that goods or services only covers apps or streaming. Conclusion By integrating these points, the DFA can close a growing fairness gap, support Europes 155million gamers, and act consistently with the democratic mandate delivered by the StopKillingGames ECI. I urge the Commission to amend the scope accordingly.
